# Cross-engine golden corpus

Query-level parity fixtures for the GQL engine. Each `*.json` file holds an
array of records; `gql/xcheck_test.go` runs every record's `gql` query over
its named fixture graph (under both eval paths) and asserts the produced
rows equal `rows`.

The authoritative corpus is generated by the Rust engine (rustychickpeas
`tasks/200`: an `export_xcheck` example over RCPG fixture graphs) and
vendored here, mirroring the RCPG conformance-corpus lockstep convention.
Until that exporter lands, `seed/` carries a Go-generated corpus that pins
the record schema and gives the harness real coverage; regenerate it with:

    GOCHICKPEAS_XCHECK_REGEN=1 go test ./gql/ -run TestXCheckCorpus

## Record schema

```json
{
  "cypher":    "MATCH (p:Person) ... ",   // the Rust engine's query text (null until it speaks GQL)
  "gql":       "MATCH (p:Person) ... ",   // the query this engine runs (null in Rust-exported records until translated)
  "graph":     "social",                  // fixture graph name
  "params":    {"who": "Alice"},          // optional $name parameters (encoded values)
  "unordered": true,                      // optional: compare rows order-insensitively
  "columns":   ["name", "n"],
  "rows":      [["Alice", 2], ...]
}
```

A record whose `gql` field is null is skipped (untranslated). Graph
resolution: a `<name>.rcpg` file next to the JSON wins (the Rust-exported
form); otherwise `<name>` must be one of the harness's built-in seed
builders (`social`, `replies`, `weighted`, `geo`).

## Value encoding

Chosen so both engines can emit and compare byte-canonical JSON:

- null -> JSON `null`; booleans -> JSON booleans; strings -> JSON strings.
- integers -> JSON numbers in integer form (decode with json.Number /
  serde_json arbitrary precision; i64-exact).
- floats -> `{"float": "<s>"}` where `<s>` is the shortest decimal that
  round-trips the f64 (Go `strconv.FormatFloat(f, 'g', -1, 64)`; Rust
  `format!("{f}")` differs on exponents, so the exporter must emit the
  Go form or bit-normalize). NaN/infinities: `"NaN"`, `"+Inf"`, `"-Inf"`.
- nodes -> `{"node": id}`; relationships -> `{"rel": pos}`.
- temporals -> `{"temporal": millis, "kind": 0|1|2}` (date, datetime,
  localdatetime).
- durations -> `{"duration": [months, days, millis]}`.
- lists -> JSON arrays; paths -> `{"path": {"nodes": [...], "rels": [...]}}`.
- maps -> `{"map": [[key, value], ...]}` with entries in ascending key
  order (map equality is insertion-order-independent, so the encoding
  canonicalizes).

Rows are compared in produced order; with `"unordered": true` both sides
sort rows by their canonical encoding first. Determinism rules for the
exporter: no time or randomness outside pinned seeds; queries whose result
order is unspecified must set `unordered`.
